model_summary: This initial gVAR-ready observation model targets six core burnout-related criteria (C01–C06) with six modifiable predictors (P01–P06) covering cognitive self-regulation, digital boundaries, boundary setting, boundary enforcement, circadian/social alignment, and attention/notification management. Measurements rely on brief momentary EMA items (1–9 Likert) collected 4 times per day over a 14-day period, plus a concise daily diary anchor. The design emphasizes observable, non-redundant constructs with likely within-person variance and mitigated collinearity, enabling early estimation of cross-lagged relationships and salutogenic progress toward a rested, better-regulated state.
variable_selection_notes: Variables were chosen to reflect the complaint’s core dynamics: persistent workload during rest (C01), lack of restoration (C02), guilt around rest (C03), perceived loss of control (C04), boundary-implementation difficulty (C05), and social minimization (C06). Predictors map one-to-one to each criterion to reduce redundancy, ensure interpretability, and support targeted intervention planning (e.g., stopping threat monitoring, digital boundaries, boundary planning, adaptive coping, circadian adjustments, and notification management). Bias considerations: self-report EMA susceptibility to social desirability is mitigated by brief, frequent prompts; however, high within-person variance is expected. Uncertainty/trade-offs: a compact set of 6 predictors may miss weaker cross-domain effects, but enhances estimability and actionability for initial gVAR modeling.

momentary_ema.notes: 4 prompts/day across 14 days; jitter within blocks to avoid predictability.

daily_diary.notes: Optional anchor diary to contextualize sleep/rest state.

passive_streams.notes: Passive streams not used in this initial model to reduce complexity.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: 6 predictors × 6 criteria form a compact yet informative network; plan for z-scored within-person transformations.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Expect meaningful within-person variance with EMA; moderate between-person differences in baseline burnout.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Send reminder prompts and provide brief EMA completions incentives; consider data-quality checks and imputation readiness.

safety_notes: Not a diagnosis. No medical dosing. If acute risk is identified in any data review, follow appropriate safety protocols and refer to care as needed.

criteria_variables.label: Work activity during designated rest

measurement.item_or_signal: Engages in work-related actions (e.g., checking or responding to work email, doing tasks) during intended days off or other designated rest periods.
measurement.notes: Captures impulsive work-related behaviors during rest periods.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / substance-related and addictive disorders / non-substance-related (behavioral) addictive disorders / work addiction (workaholism) / inability_to_detach_from_work
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / substance-related and addictive disorders / non-substance-related (behavioral) addictive disorders / work addiction (workaholism) / *inability_to_detach_from_work
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label Engages in work-related actions during intended rest periods directly maps to boundary failure/detachment from work. The best-matching leaf is 'inability_to_detach_from_work' (idx=21953) in DSM-5TR, which operationalizes not detaching from work during off-hours, aligning with the EMA momentary item capturing work activity during designated rest.
criteria_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Work activity during designated rest | Operational item/signal: Engages in work-related actions (e.g., checking or responding to work email, doing tasks) during intended days off or other designated rest periods. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures impulsive work-related behaviors during rest periods.

criteria_variables.label: Lack of perceived restoration

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now or in the recent moment, rest or recovery did not feel refreshing; you cannot recall last time you felt rested.
measurement.notes: Assesses subjective restoration after rest periods.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / other specified insomnia disorder / paradoxical insomnia (sleep state misperception) / subjective_sleep_quality_poor
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / other specified insomnia disorder / paradoxical insomnia (sleep state misperception) / *subjective_sleep_quality_poor
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Best match for 'Lack of perceived restoration' is the leaf 'nonrestorative_sleep_experience' (sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / sleep-maintenance insomnia / nonrestorative_sleep_experience). The variable label 'Lack of perceived restoration' and the EMA item 'Right now or in the recent moment, rest or recovery did not feel refreshing; you cannot recall last time you felt rested' directly describe subjective nonrestoration of rest. This aligns with the measurement modality (EMA self-report, momentary, smartphone prompts, recall window since last prompt) and maps to a subjective restoration construct within the sleep-wake/insomnia domain rather than circadian phase or other domains.
criteria_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Lack of perceived restoration | Operational item/signal: Right now or in the recent moment, rest or recovery did not feel refreshing; you cannot recall last time you felt rested. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses subjective restoration after rest periods.

criteria_variables.label: Guilt when attempting to rest

measurement.item_or_signal: Experiences guilt or self-reproach when not working or when taking time off, which occurs during rest periods.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / depressive disorders / other specified depressive disorder / recurrent brief depression / guilt_present
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / depressive disorders / other specified depressive disorder / recurrent brief depression / *guilt_present
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label corresponds to 'Guilt when attempting to rest' and EMA self-report captures momentary guilt during rest periods. The best-matching leaf is guilt_present (clinical depression-related emotional state) among candidates, aligning with the cognitive-emotional cost of resting. Although clinical in scope, this leaf most directly represents the momentary guilt experience targeted by C03, supported by measurement item (Experiences guilt or self-reproach when not working) and EMA sampling (4 prompts/day, since_last_prompt) indicating within-person variance. Other guilt leaves either specify excessive/worthlessness guilt or are about releasing/overcoming guilt, which are downstream/therapeutic constructs rather than the immediate momentary guilt signal intended by C03.
criteria_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Guilt when attempting to rest | Operational item/signal: Experiences guilt or self-reproach when not working or when taking time off, which occurs during rest periods. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures cognitive-emotional cost of resting.

criteria_variables.label: Perceived inability to stop working

measurement.item_or_signal: Feels unable to stop work-related activity despite wanting or needing a break; reports lack of control over continuing work.
measurement.notes: Assesses perceived control over work engagement.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_full_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / substance-related and addictive disorders / non-substance-related (behavioral) addictive disorders / work addiction (workaholism) / inability_to_detach_from_work
criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: CLINICAL / DSM-5TR / substance-related and addictive disorders / non-substance-related (behavioral) addictive disorders / work addiction (workaholism) / *inability_to_detach_from_work
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable C04 maps to perceived loss of control over continuing work. The measurement item 'Feels unable to stop work-related activity...' on a 1-9 EMA scale via smartphone app directly assesses inability to detach from work, matching the 'inability_to_detach_from_work' leaf. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates, aligning with boundary enforcement and rest-related goals described in the pseudoprofile; EMA context (momentary, 4 prompts/day) supports within-person variance and actionable cross-domain modeling. Other leaves describe preoccupation, time spent thinking, or consequences, but none capture the explicit control/detachment dynamic as precisely as this leaf.
criteria_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Perceived inability to stop working | Operational item/signal: Feels unable to stop work-related activity despite wanting or needing a break; reports lack of control over continuing work. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses perceived control over work engagement.

criteria_variables.label: Difficulty implementing boundaries

measurement.item_or_signal: Has trouble creating or practicing concrete boundary strategies (e.g., turning off notifications).

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / psychological / autonomy / boundary setting / establishing limits / *enforcing limits
criteria_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable C05 corresponds to boundary-implementation difficulty. The measure targets concrete boundary strategies; this aligns with boundary-setting and enforcement. Among candidates, idx 3861 'establishing limits / enforcing limits' most precisely captures boundary-implementation difficulty (establishing and enforcing personal limits), fitting the EMA-based self-report of boundary management. Other leaves focus more on personal time, emotional boundaries, or definitional aspects rather than the actionable enforcement/maintenance of boundaries.
criteria_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Difficulty implementing boundaries | Operational item/signal: Has trouble creating or practicing concrete boundary strategies (e.g., turning off notifications). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures self-regulatory boundary implementation difficulty.

criteria_variables.label: Perceived minimization by social network

measurement.item_or_signal: In recent interactions, to what extent did friends minimize or oversimplify your burnout when you discussed it?
measurement.notes: Assesses perceived social support vs minimization.

criteria_variables.mapped_leaf_embed_path: NON-CLINICAL / AGE-GENERAL / social / social integration / social connectedness / network of friends / social support from friends / appraisal support / *feedback

The item asks how much friends minimized or oversimplified burnout when discussed, which fits appraisal support / feedback as the mechanism. Among candidates, idx 2981 is the most specific leaf: 'appraisal support / feedback'. This provides the best operational counterpart for the measurement item and the social mechanism implied by the variable label, aligning with the pseudoprofile’s focus on social minimization as a core criterion.

predictor_variables.label: Stop threat monitoring and checking
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Directly reduces compulsive checking and threat monitoring linked to burnout.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Cognitive strategies to reduce scanning for threats; limit nonessential monitoring during rest.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you stop or reduce threat monitoring and checking (e.g., checking work emails) when attempting rest?

expected_effects.rationale: Greater stop of threat monitoring expected to reduce work-related rest intrusion.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best match is idx 2543: threat_monitoring_reduction_plan under PSYCHO; directly represents a metacognitive/attentional intervention to stop/reduce threat monitoring and checking. The var label Stop threat_monitoring and checking and the EMA item 'In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you stop or reduce threat monitoring and checking (e.g., checking work emails) when attempting rest?' align with a reduction-plan mechanism; it's more specific than other CBT/CBT-based leaves which address beliefs, worry, or general behaviors.

predictor_variables.label: Digital boundaries and recovery plan (digital hygiene)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Directly governs exposure to digital intrusions, enabling restoration.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Turn off nonessential notifications; set device-free windows; establish recovery-friendly digital routines.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement the digital overload recovery plan (e.g., notifications off during rest periods)?

expected_effects.rationale: Stronger boundary practices should enhance restoration perceptions.

feasibility.notes: Moderate participant burden but high salience.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-match leaf for P02: Digital boundaries and recovery plan (digital hygiene). The candidate leaf exactly matches the label; path, and measurement item: SOCIAL / digital_social_environment / digital_social_environment_solutions / boundaries_and_wellbeing / digital_overload_recovery_plan with an EMA item asking about implementation over the past 24 hours. Mechanism aligns with reducing digital intrusions to enable restoration, fitting the stated measurement and context (4 prompts/day).

predictor_variables.label: Boundary with external stressors plan
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Boundary-setting with others reduces external demands, supporting rest.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Precommitment to set limits in conversations; scheduled days off; communicated expectations.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement boundary plans with external stressors (e.g., people, tasks)?

expected_effects.rationale: Better boundary planning reduces guilt associated with rest.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Exact label match to boundary_with_external_stressors_plan leaf. It directly corresponds to boundary planning/enforcement with external stressors, matching the variable name 'Boundary with external stressors plan' and the measurement item 'In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement boundary plans with external stressors'. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates and aligns with EMA momentary assessment of boundary setting to rest.

predictor_variables.label: Reduce time checking behavior
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Directly targets time spent checking, easing rest intrusion.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Set fixed check times; use scheduled email blocks; practice nonchecking during rest.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you reduce time spent checking work emails or messages?

expected_effects.rationale: Less checking behavior should correlate with greater resting control.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best-matching leaf is 3826: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / sleep_related_cognitive_behavioral_interventions / conditioning_and_associations / relearning / reduce_time_checking_behavior. It directly encodes reducing time spent checking behavior, aligning with the variable P04 and its measurement item (past 24 hours, extent of reduced checking) and mechanism (conditioning/relearning to reduce checking to protect/rest). Other candidates address related concepts (threat monitoring, reassurance-seeking, or general behavioral strategies) but are less specific to the exact construct and sleep/rest-oriented conditioning implied by P04.

predictor_variables.label: Weekend social shift mitigation (circadian support)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Adjusting weekend social load supports sleep and recovery.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Plan lighter social load on weekends; align social activities with sleep goals.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you adjust weekend social plans to support recovery (sleep/rest)?

expected_effects.rationale: Weekend plan adjustments may improve restoration perceptions.

feasibility.notes: Slightly more complex due to weekend variability.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Closest and exact leaf mapping: variable P05 corresponds to weekend social shift mitigation under Circadian Entrainment within Sleep Circadian and Restoration, matching the leaf path BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Circadian_Entraining / context_shift_plans / weekend_social_shift_mitigation. The measurement item assesses past 24 hours’ adjustment of weekend social plans to support recovery (sleep/rest) via momentary EMA, aligning with the described mechanism of circadian-supportive social scheduling. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this predictor.
predictor_variables.mapping_query_text_used: Weekend social shift mitigation (circadian support) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Adjusting weekend social load supports sleep and recovery. | Intervention examples: Plan lighter social load on weekends; align social activities with sleep goals. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you adjust weekend social plans to support recovery (sleep/rest)? | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Circadian_Entraining / context_shift_plans / *weekend_social_shift_mitigation

predictor_variables.label: Notification and attention management plan
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Directly reduces attentional intrusion and supports self-regulation.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Enable focus modes; schedule notification checks; minimize multitasking.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement your notification and attention management plan (e.g., reduced notifications, scheduled checks)?
measurement.notes: Assesses pragmatic boundary management for attention.

expected_effects.rationale: Better notification management likely reduces perceived social minimization via reduced distress talk.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Best match is the leaf 'notification_and_attention_management_plan' (idx 640) which exactly corresponds to the variable label 'Notification and attention management plan'. It uses the EMA item 'In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement your notification and attention management plan', a 1–9 Likert scale, with sampling 4 times per day and past_24_hours recall, aligning directly with the measurement item. Mechanistically, this predictor directly reduces attentional intrusion and supports self-regulation, which fits the intended boundary/attention management function described in the pseudoprofile. The pseudoprofile context emphasizes boundary enforcement and notification management, supporting this mapping as the most specific operational artifact among candidates.

measurement.item_or_signal: Engages in work-related actions (e.g., checking or responding to work email, doing tasks) during intended days off or other designated rest periods.
measurement.notes: Captures impulsive work-related behaviors during rest periods.

criteria.label: Lack of perceived restoration
criteria.query_text_used: Lack of perceived restoration | Operational item/signal: Right now or in the recent moment, rest or recovery did not feel refreshing; you cannot recall last time you felt rested. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses subjective restoration after rest periods.

mapping.rationale: Best match for 'Lack of perceived restoration' is the leaf 'nonrestorative_sleep_experience' (sleep-wake disorders / insomnia disorders / sleep-maintenance insomnia / nonrestorative_sleep_experience). The variable label 'Lack of perceived restoration' and the EMA item 'Right now or in the recent moment, rest or recovery did not feel refreshing; you cannot recall last time you felt rested' directly describe subjective nonrestoration of rest. This aligns with the measurement modality (EMA self-report, momentary, smartphone prompts, recall window since last prompt) and maps to a subjective restoration construct within the sleep-wake/insomnia domain rather than circadian phase or other domains.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now or in the recent moment, rest or recovery did not feel refreshing; you cannot recall last time you felt rested.
measurement.notes: Assesses subjective restoration after rest periods.

criteria.label: Guilt when attempting to rest
criteria.query_text_used: Guilt when attempting to rest | Operational item/signal: Experiences guilt or self-reproach when not working or when taking time off, which occurs during rest periods. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures cognitive-emotional cost of resting.

mapping.rationale: Variable label corresponds to 'Guilt when attempting to rest' and EMA self-report captures momentary guilt during rest periods. The best-matching leaf is guilt_present (clinical depression-related emotional state) among candidates, aligning with the cognitive-emotional cost of resting. Although clinical in scope, this leaf most directly represents the momentary guilt experience targeted by C03, supported by measurement item (Experiences guilt or self-reproach when not working) and EMA sampling (4 prompts/day, since_last_prompt) indicating within-person variance. Other guilt leaves either specify excessive/worthlessness guilt or are about releasing/overcoming guilt, which are downstream/therapeutic constructs rather than the immediate momentary guilt signal intended by C03.

measurement.item_or_signal: Experiences guilt or self-reproach when not working or when taking time off, which occurs during rest periods.

criteria.label: Perceived inability to stop working
criteria.query_text_used: Perceived inability to stop working | Operational item/signal: Feels unable to stop work-related activity despite wanting or needing a break; reports lack of control over continuing work. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses perceived control over work engagement.

mapping.rationale: Variable C04 maps to perceived loss of control over continuing work. The measurement item 'Feels unable to stop work-related activity...' on a 1-9 EMA scale via smartphone app directly assesses inability to detach from work, matching the 'inability_to_detach_from_work' leaf. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates, aligning with boundary enforcement and rest-related goals described in the pseudoprofile; EMA context (momentary, 4 prompts/day) supports within-person variance and actionable cross-domain modeling. Other leaves describe preoccupation, time spent thinking, or consequences, but none capture the explicit control/detachment dynamic as precisely as this leaf.

measurement.notes: Assesses perceived control over work engagement.

criteria.label: Difficulty implementing boundaries
criteria.query_text_used: Difficulty implementing boundaries | Operational item/signal: Has trouble creating or practicing concrete boundary strategies (e.g., turning off notifications). | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Captures self-regulatory boundary implementation difficulty.

mapping.rationale: Variable C05 corresponds to boundary-implementation difficulty. The measure targets concrete boundary strategies; this aligns with boundary-setting and enforcement. Among candidates, idx 3861 'establishing limits / enforcing limits' most precisely captures boundary-implementation difficulty (establishing and enforcing personal limits), fitting the EMA-based self-report of boundary management. Other leaves focus more on personal time, emotional boundaries, or definitional aspects rather than the actionable enforcement/maintenance of boundaries.

criteria.label: Perceived minimization by social network
criteria.query_text_used: Perceived minimization by social network | Operational item/signal: In recent interactions, to what extent did friends minimize or oversimplify your burnout when you discussed it? | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Assesses perceived social support vs minimization.

mapping.rationale: C06 measures perceived minimization of burnout in social interactions. The item asks how much friends minimized or oversimplified burnout when discussed, which fits appraisal support / feedback as the mechanism. Among candidates, idx 2981 is the most specific leaf: 'appraisal support / feedback'. This provides the best operational counterpart for the measurement item and the social mechanism implied by the variable label, aligning with the pseudoprofile’s focus on social minimization as a core criterion.

measurement.item_or_signal: In recent interactions, to what extent did friends minimize or oversimplify your burnout when you discussed it?
measurement.notes: Assesses perceived social support vs minimization.

predictors.label: Stop threat monitoring and checking
predictors.query_text_used: Stop threat monitoring and checking | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Directly reduces compulsive checking and threat monitoring linked to burnout. | Intervention examples: Cognitive strategies to reduce scanning for threats; limit nonessential monitoring during rest. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you stop or reduce threat monitoring and checking (e.g., checking work emails) when attempting rest? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / Cognitive_Behavioral_Therapies / Metacognitive_and_Attention_Based_Interventions / Stop_threat_monitoring_and_checking

mapping.rationale: Best match is idx 2543: threat_monitoring_reduction_plan under PSYCHO; directly represents a metacognitive/attentional intervention to stop/reduce threat monitoring and checking. The var label Stop threat_monitoring and checking and the EMA item 'In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you stop or reduce threat monitoring and checking (e.g., checking work emails) when attempting rest?' align with a reduction-plan mechanism; it's more specific than other CBT/CBT-based leaves which address beliefs, worry, or general behaviors.

source_variable.label: Stop threat monitoring and checking
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Directly reduces compulsive checking and threat monitoring linked to burnout.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Cognitive strategies to reduce scanning for threats; limit nonessential monitoring during rest.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you stop or reduce threat monitoring and checking (e.g., checking work emails) when attempting rest?

expected_effects.rationale: Greater stop of threat monitoring expected to reduce work-related rest intrusion.

mapping.rationale: Best-match leaf for P02: Digital boundaries and recovery plan (digital hygiene). The candidate leaf exactly matches the label; path, and measurement item: SOCIAL / digital_social_environment / digital_social_environment_solutions / boundaries_and_wellbeing / digital_overload_recovery_plan with an EMA item asking about implementation over the past 24 hours. source_variable.label: Digital boundaries and recovery plan (digital hygiene)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Directly governs exposure to digital intrusions, enabling restoration.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Turn off nonessential notifications; set device-free windows; establish recovery-friendly digital routines.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement the digital overload recovery plan (e.g., notifications off during rest periods)?

expected_effects.rationale: Stronger boundary practices should enhance restoration perceptions.

feasibility.notes: Moderate participant burden but high salience.

predictors.label: Boundary with external stressors plan
predictors.query_text_used: Boundary with external stressors plan | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Boundary-setting with others reduces external demands, supporting rest. | Intervention examples: Precommitment to set limits in conversations; scheduled days off; communicated expectations. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement boundary plans with external stressors (e.g., people, tasks)? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / boundary_planning / *boundary_with_external_stressors_plan

mapping.rationale: Exact label match to boundary_with_external_stressors_plan leaf. It directly corresponds to boundary planning/enforcement with external stressors, matching the variable name 'Boundary with external stressors plan' and the measurement item 'In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement boundary plans with external stressors'. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates and aligns with EMA momentary assessment of boundary setting to rest.

source_variable.label: Boundary with external stressors plan
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Boundary-setting with others reduces external demands, supporting rest.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Precommitment to set limits in conversations; scheduled days off; communicated expectations.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement boundary plans with external stressors (e.g., people, tasks)?

expected_effects.rationale: Better boundary planning reduces guilt associated with rest.

predictors.label: Reduce time checking behavior
predictors.query_text_used: Reduce time checking behavior | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Directly targets time spent checking, easing rest intrusion. | Intervention examples: Set fixed check times; use scheduled email blocks; practice nonchecking during rest. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you reduce time spent checking work emails or messages? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / sleep_related_cognitive_behavioral_interventions / conditioning_and_associations / relearning / *reduce_time_checking_behavior

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is 3826: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / sleep_related_cognitive_behavioral_interventions / conditioning_and_associations / relearning / reduce_time_checking_behavior. It directly encodes reducing time spent checking behavior, aligning with the variable P04 and its measurement item (past 24 hours, extent of reduced checking) and mechanism (conditioning/relearning to reduce checking to protect/rest). Other candidates address related concepts (threat monitoring, reassurance-seeking, or general behavioral strategies) but are less specific to the exact construct and sleep/rest-oriented conditioning implied by P04.

source_variable.label: Reduce time checking behavior
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Directly targets time spent checking, easing rest intrusion.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Set fixed check times; use scheduled email blocks; practice nonchecking during rest.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you reduce time spent checking work emails or messages?

expected_effects.rationale: Less checking behavior should correlate with greater resting control.

predictors.label: Weekend social shift mitigation (circadian support)
predictors.query_text_used: Weekend social shift mitigation (circadian support) | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Adjusting weekend social load supports sleep and recovery. | Intervention examples: Plan lighter social load on weekends; align social activities with sleep goals. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you adjust weekend social plans to support recovery (sleep/rest)? | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Circadian_Entraining / context_shift_plans / *weekend_social_shift_mitigation

mapping.rationale: Closest and exact leaf mapping: variable P05 corresponds to weekend social shift mitigation under Circadian Entrainment within Sleep Circadian and Restoration, matching the leaf path BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Circadian_Entraining / context_shift_plans / weekend_social_shift_mitigation. The measurement item assesses past 24 hours’ adjustment of weekend social plans to support recovery (sleep/rest) via momentary EMA, aligning with the described mechanism of circadian-supportive social scheduling. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this predictor.

source_variable.label: Weekend social shift mitigation (circadian support)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Adjusting weekend social load supports sleep and recovery.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Plan lighter social load on weekends; align social activities with sleep goals.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you adjust weekend social plans to support recovery (sleep/rest)?

expected_effects.rationale: Weekend plan adjustments may improve restoration perceptions.

feasibility.notes: Slightly more complex due to weekend variability.

predictors.label: Notification and attention management plan
predictors.query_text_used: Notification and attention management plan | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Directly reduces attentional intrusion and supports self-regulation. | Intervention examples: Enable focus modes; schedule notification checks; minimize multitasking. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement your notification and attention management plan (e.g., reduced notifications, scheduled checks)? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / behavioral_integration / boundaries_and_attention_management / *notification_and_attention_management_plan

mapping.rationale: Best match is the leaf 'notification_and_attention_management_plan' (idx 640) which exactly corresponds to the variable label 'Notification and attention management plan'. It uses the EMA item 'In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement your notification and attention management plan', a 1–9 Likert scale, with sampling 4 times per day and past_24_hours recall, aligning directly with the measurement item. Mechanistically, this predictor directly reduces attentional intrusion and supports self-regulation, which fits the intended boundary/attention management function described in the pseudoprofile. The pseudoprofile context emphasizes boundary enforcement and notification management, supporting this mapping as the most specific operational artifact among candidates.

source_variable.label: Notification and attention management plan
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Directly reduces attentional intrusion and supports self-regulation.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Enable focus modes; schedule notification checks; minimize multitasking.

measurement.item_or_signal: In the past 24 hours, to what extent did you implement your notification and attention management plan (e.g., reduced notifications, scheduled checks)?
measurement.notes: Assesses pragmatic boundary management for attention.

expected_effects.rationale: Better notification management likely reduces perceived social minimization via reduced distress talk.
